message.content:
Adversaries may employ VBA Stomping to conceal malicious Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) payloads within MS Office documents by replacing the VBA source code with benign or random data. This technique exploits the fact that MS Office documents store both the VBA source code and a compiled version known as p-code. By overwriting the VBA source code with non-malicious content while retaining the harmful p-code, adversaries can evade detection by tools that scan for malicious VBA source code. If the MS Office version specified in the _VBA_PROJECT stream matches the host application, the malicious p-code is executed. Otherwise, the benign source code is recompiled, potentially bypassing dynamic analysis.

The impact of this technique includes the execution of hidden malicious code, leading to unauthorized actions such as data exfiltration or system compromise. Common exploitation methods involve embedding the malicious p-code in documents shared via email or other means.

Mitigation strategies include using advanced security tools capable of detecting anomalies in p-code, implementing strict email and document handling policies, and educating users about the risks of opening unsolicited attachments. Regular updates and patches to MS Office applications can also help mitigate vulnerabilities exploited by this technique.
